• Absence of the diaphragm in the lower posterior region of the heart, consistent with diaphragmatic hernia  
• Right lung hypoplasia with a small pleural effusion  
• Possible esophageal atresia with tracheoesophageal fistula  
• Suggested interruption of continuity in the cervical vertebral bodies  

At 23 weeks, three-dimensional ultrasound with Crystal Vue revealed:
• A defect in the middle and posterior diaphragm allowing partial herniation of abdominal organs into the chest  
• A markedly shortened esophagus (1.5 cm in length)  
• The distal esophagus directly connected to a cystic mass, confirming an intrathoracic stomach
differential_diagnoses: ventral cystic mass in the thoracic cavity

case_text:
A 31-year-old man presented with a 6-day history of severe headache and vomiting. He reported a progressive loss of vision in his left eye and decreased libido over the preceding two years. He denied any rhinorrhea, trauma, or prior neurosurgery. On examination, he was alert, febrile (38.0 °C), and had complete blindness of the left eye without other cranial-nerve deficits; no nasal discharge was noted.

Laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein level of 305 mg/L (normal <10 mg/L), a leukocyte count of 13,000/μL, serum sodium 131 mEq/L, and blood glucose 127 mg/dL. Hormonal assays revealed a prolactin level of 9000 ng/mL (normal 2.5–11 ng/mL) with additional findings consistent with panhypopituitarism.

Computed tomography of the head demonstrated a large sellar mass with erosion of the sellar floor and extension into the sphenoid sinus. Magnetic resonance imaging confirmed a suprasellar and infrasellar lesion extending into the cavernous sinuses, encroaching on the third ventricle, and compressing the optic chiasm, without evidence of intratumoral hemorrhage.

A lumbar puncture yielded purulent cerebrospinal fluid containing 11,900 white blood cells/μL, 2,680 red blood cells/μL, protein 6.3 g/L, glucose 39 mg/dL, and lactate 81 mg/dL.  
What is your differential diagnosis?

A 24-year-old man with no prior medical history presented with nonradiating central chest pain of a few hours’ duration. One day earlier, he had been diagnosed with group A streptococcal pharyngitis and started on amoxicillin 500 mg orally twice daily. On initial evaluation, his heart rate was 116 beats per minute; other vital signs were normal. In urgent care, an electrocardiogram showed sinus bradycardia with T-wave inversions in leads III, aVR, V1, and V2 and ST-segment depressions in leads aVR and V1. He was transferred to the emergency department, where bradycardia persisted but other vital signs remained unremarkable. A single dose of aspirin 324 mg was given. Laboratory testing revealed an elevated troponin T concentration. A heparin infusion was initiated, and cardiology was consulted. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated only trace tricuspid and mitral regurgitation. Repeat ECGs were unchanged, and no evidence of acute coronary syndrome was seen.

A 60-year-old man presented with a six-week history of sharp, left-sided chest pain worsened by inspiration and lying down. He also reported progressive back pain exacerbated by walking, constipation, and lower-limb weakness and numbness suggestive of spinal cord compression. He had no history of smoking or significant past medical conditions. On examination, vital signs were stable. There was abdominal distension without hepatomegaly, minimal lower-limb weakness, and otherwise normal cardiovascular and respiratory findings.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 138 g/L, white-cell count 5.14×10^9/L with a normal differential, platelets 256×10^9/L, normal renal function, and normal serum calcium. Contrast MRI of the spine revealed widespread skeletal lesions with at least three large thoracic paraspinal soft-tissue deposits, including one at T4/T5 causing significant cord compression. A chest radiograph demonstrated a left upper-lobe opacity. Contrast CT of the chest confirmed a 75 mm lobulated mass in the left upper lobe with invasion into the chest wall (involving the left third rib and adjacent muscle), a mild to moderate left pleural effusion, and CT evidence of vertebral and pelvic bony lesions.

A 20-year-old woman was referred for constant right proximal thigh pain that had progressed over 8 years. She had no other medical conditions, menarche at age 13, and no family history of bone disease. The pain was worse at night, somewhat relieved by activity and cold baths, and required daily NSAIDs, paracetamol, and weak opioids. She had a leg-length discrepancy managed with shoe inserts and was on sick leave due to her symptoms. At age 11 she had hip joint pain with an ultrasound-guided aspiration revealing sterile fluid; pain resolved. Six months prior, she was hit by a car at knee level, with no fracture diagnosed, after which her thigh pain worsened.

On examination, there were no skin lesions, café au lait spots, erythema, heat, or swelling over the right thigh. Leg lengths were slightly unequal but within normal limits.

Magnetic resonance imaging and PET–CT showed significant increased cortical thickness of the right femoral diaphysis with partial medullary obliteration and an uneven sclerotic surface. A Tc-99m bone scan demonstrated increased uptake in most of the right femoral shaft. Biochemical bone markers, including alkaline phosphatase, were normal. Genetic screening in blood for the GNAS1 R201 mutation and for SQSTM1 mutations was negative. Bone biopsy of the lesion revealed thickened bone trabeculae with preserved lamellar structure, accentuated cement lines, and no fibrosis, inflammation, alcian positivity, malignancy, or neoplasia.

An 84-year-old woman had an incidentally discovered simple liver cyst one year earlier. She was asymptomatic, and physical examination was unremarkable. Liver function tests were normal, and hepatitis B and C serologies were negative. Serum carcinoembryonic antigen was 5.0 ng/mL and CA19-9 was 43.5 U/mL. Contrast-enhanced CT showed a well-defined, low-attenuation cystic lesion that increased in maximal diameter from 41 to 47 mm over one year, with an irregularly shaped mural nodule demonstrating progressive contrast enhancement and no biliary duct dilation. Enhanced ultrasonography revealed a 14 mm isoechoic irregular nodule with a distinct feeding artery and gradual enhancement. MRI depicted a homogeneous water-intensity cystic lesion; the mural nodule showed peripheral low intensity and central high intensity on heavily T2-weighted images and contrast enhancement on portal venous–phase T1-weighted images comparable to intrahepatic vessels, with hyperintensity on diffusion-weighted imaging. PET/CT demonstrated no abnormal ^18F-fluorodeoxyglucose uptake in the nodule.

A 56‐year‐old woman with rheumatoid arthritis and a history of multiple myeloma treated by bone marrow transplantation presented with a painful ulcer on the lower leg clinically consistent with pyoderma gangrenosum (PG). A biopsy showed a benign ulcer with a dense neutrophilic infiltrate, and stains and cultures for bacteria, acid‐fast bacteria, and fungi were negative. Over the next decade, she had recurrent PG flares on her extremities managed with systemic and intralesional steroids, adalimumab, cyclosporine, mycophenolate, and anakinra, during which her rheumatoid arthritis also improved.

Ten years after her initial PG diagnosis, she was hospitalized with new shortness of breath and worsening cutaneous PG. She was afebrile. Laboratory studies showed a C‐reactive protein level of 14 mg/L and a leukocyte count of 17,000 per cubic millimeter with 80% neutrophils. Contrast‐enhanced computed tomography revealed a moderate pericardial effusion and multiple large, bilateral, cavitary pulmonary lesions as well as splenic abscesses. She was treated empirically with broad‐spectrum antibiotics and her immunosuppressive therapies were discontinued, after which her clinical status deteriorated.

Mycobacterium fortuitum was isolated from a sputum sample. Bronchoalveolar lavage, transbronchial biopsy, and two lung wedge resections showed only abscess formation with no evidence of infection by stains, cultures, or molecular studies. An antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody test was negative. Despite initiation of broad‐spectrum antibiotics and systemic antifungal therapy, her condition continued to worsen.

A 53-year-old man was referred for rehabilitation of mandibular opening after life-saving surgery and reconstruction for a gunshot wound to the face. He had undergone palatal defect repair with a tongue pedicle flap and open reduction of a comminuted condylar fracture, followed by placement of a prosthetic left temporomandibular joint. His maximal interincisal opening improved from 5 mm to 28 mm with physical therapy.

During the initial evaluation, he reported a second complaint of headache. The headache was strictly left‐sided, located periorbitally and in the temporal region, described as a constant background ache that waxed and waned, with superimposed severe exacerbations occurring unpredictably. During these exacerbations, he experienced ipsilateral lacrimation, persistent rhinorrhea, and photophobia.

He also reported a burning and tingling sensation over the left zygomatic arch. On examination, the left forehead was flat with a lowered eyebrow. The left eyelid was ptotic, and anisocoria was present with a smaller left pupil. The left conjunctiva appeared injected. Allodynia and hyperalgesia were noted in the distribution of the zygomatic branch of the trigeminal nerve on the left side.

A 75-year-old woman presented with acute severe dyspnea. Eight and thirteen years earlier, she had undergone coronary artery bypass grafting: the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) to the left anterior descending artery, the right internal mammary artery (RIMA) to an obtuse marginal branch, and a saphenous vein graft to the distal left circumflex artery. Her medical history included hypertension, diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 6.5%–6.7%), hyperlipidemia (LDL 100 mg/dL), and a bilateral neck bruit noted two years ago. MR angiography at that time showed <50% stenosis of both carotid arteries and 50% stenosis of both subclavian arteries. Ankle–brachial indices were elevated (ABI 1.62 on both sides), attributed to arterial stiffness, and she was asymptomatic without inter-arm blood pressure difference or arm claudication.

Six months before admission, she developed exertional chest discomfort. Two months later, her systolic blood pressure fell from 126/70 mmHg to 94/60 mmHg. ECG showed worsening ST-segment depression and QRS widening; echocardiography revealed an ejection fraction of 40% (previously >55%). Treatment with pimobendan led to slight improvement. One week before admission, her blood pressure measured 70/40 mmHg in the right arm and 80/50 mmHg in the left arm; antihypertensive medications were discontinued.

She was scheduled for cardiac catheterization but presented first with severe dyspnea. In the emergency department, respiratory rate was 30/min, heart rate 86/min, temperature 36.0°C, and bilateral arm blood pressures were difficult to measure. Chest radiography showed severe bilateral pulmonary edema. ECG demonstrated diffuse ST-segment depression with non-specific intraventricular conduction delay. Cardiac troponin I was positive; creatine phosphokinase rose from 66 U/L to 720 U/L. Renal and liver functions were normal.

She was intubated, admitted to intensive care, and started on intravenous dobutamine and furosemide. Dyspnea improved rapidly, and urine output was 950 mL in the first 4 hours and 50–100 mL/h thereafter. Despite vigorous treatment, systolic blood pressure remained low at 60–70 mmHg in both arms, but femoral pulses were easily palpable.

A 73-year-old man with a history of treated prostate cancer presented with progressive left lower-extremity weakness and numbness over several years, chronic low back pain, and constipation. On examination, there was diminished sensation in the left groin, anterior thigh, and calf; muscle strength was 4/5 in the right lower extremity and 3/5 in the left lower extremity. He had a history of resection of a lumbar spinal tumor in 1992, originally diagnosed as a paraganglioma, followed by radiation therapy in 2002. Surveillance imaging suggested increase in the lumbar lesion over time. Computed tomography of the spine showed expansion of the spinal canal by an isodense soft-tissue mass with accompanying osseous destruction. Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated the mass to be hypointense on T1-weighted and T2-weighted imaging, with homogenous enhancement on postcontrast T1-weighted images.
primary_diagnoses: Malignant transformation of the previously resected paraganglioma

case_text:
A 40-year-old Nepali woman from the far western region presented with sudden onset left-sided weakness and difficulty speaking for 6 hours. Seven days earlier, she developed moderate-to-high–grade continuous fever without chills, accompanied by headaches, nonbilious vomiting, and generalized weakness. Despite 4 days of over-the-counter treatment, symptoms persisted, and during the last 3 days she developed shortness of breath and cough with mucoid sputum. She denied vision changes, altered mentation, chest pain, abdominal pain, or dysuria.

On examination, she was confused with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 14 (E4 V4 M6); temperature 102 °F; blood pressure 80/50 mmHg; heart rate 123/min; and oxygen saturation 78% on room air. She was pale and icteric, with bilateral chest crepitations. Head-to-toe inspection revealed a brownish-black scab on the right buttock. Pupils were equal and reactive; plantar responses were normal; and there was no neck rigidity.

Noncontrast CT of the head showed an acute right frontal lobe parenchymal hemorrhage with vasogenic edema and mild mass effect. Chest radiography demonstrated bilateral pulmonary infiltrates and features of pulmonary edema. Ultrasonography of the abdomen and pelvis revealed diffuse gallbladder wall thickening, borderline splenomegaly, and right-sided pleural effusion.

Laboratory studies on admission included: leukocyte count 13,200/µL (neutrophils 88%); hemoglobin 10 g/dL; platelets 460,000/µL; serum creatinine 1.7 mg/dL; total bilirubin 6.2 mg/dL (direct 2.7 mg/dL); AST 228.6 IU/L; ALT 72.3 IU/L; ALP 619.9 IU/L; arterial blood gas pH 7.26, pCO₂ 31 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 15.8 mmol/L, lactate 5.54 mmol/L.

Serological tests for dengue, leptospirosis, and Brucella were negative. Thick and thin blood smears for malaria and PCR testing for SARS-CoV-2 were negative. Sputum AFB staining and culture were negative for tuberculosis, and HIV serology was negative.

A 50-year-old Chinese woman with a history of Graves’ disease presented with one week of dyspnea and cough without fever, chest pain, or hemoptysis. She was a non-smoker, with no weight loss or appetite change. Physical examination revealed a BMI of 17 kg/m2, blood pressure 172/102 mmHg, heart rate 97 bpm, respiratory rate 20/min, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air. Chest examination showed reduced air entry on the right. Cutaneous examination disclosed bilateral small erythematous papules on the cheeks and nasolabial folds. There was no clubbing or lymphadenopathy.

A chest radiograph demonstrated a large right pneumothorax without mediastinal shift and diffuse reticular-nodular opacities in the left lung. A right‐sided chest tube was inserted with subsequent lung re-expansion and removal of the tube.

High-resolution CT of the chest revealed multiple round thin-walled cysts throughout both lungs, mild interlobular septal thickening, and numerous tiny (<4 mm) randomly distributed ground‐glass and solid nodules. Abdominal CT showed multiple fat-density lesions in both kidneys consistent with angiomyolipomas.

A 13-year-old boy with a history of asthma presented with a 3-month history of progressive lower extremity weakness and balance problems, resulting in multiple falls and the need to hold onto objects to walk. There was no history of trauma, intercurrent illness, or new medications.  
On examination, he had a positive Romberg sign, weakness of hip flexion and ankle dorsiflexion, intact reflexes, and no dysmetria or tremor.  
MRI of the spine showed a mass centered in the T10 vertebral body, extending into the posterior elements, epidural space, and paraspinal region. The lesion was heterogeneously T2 hyperintense with multiple serpentine T2 hypointense areas (suggestive of flow voids), demonstrated avid contrast enhancement, and showed facilitated diffusion. There was severe spinal canal narrowing with cord compression and T2 hyperintensity within the cord, as well as narrowing of the bilateral T10/T11 neuroforamina. No other spinal lesions were identified.  
Subsequent CT of the thoracic spine confirmed a T10 lesion with thickened, striated trabeculae, scattered areas of osteolysis, asymmetric enlargement of the T10 segmental arteries, and hypertrophy of paravertebral veins. CT of the neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no additional lesions.  
Given these findings, what is your leading diagnosis and what alternatives should be considered?

A 4-year-old girl was referred with gross hematuria and altered mental status after 6 hours of symptoms. Two days earlier she was well; she had sudden abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, and three episodes of vomiting. Her mother had given her two tablespoons of areca nut powder and ibuprofen syrup for presumed diarrhea and fever. Immediately thereafter, she became pale, developed cold extremities, gross hematuria, and two generalized tonic–clonic seizures (10 and 3 minutes in duration). On arrival, temperature was 39.4°C, heart rate 140/min, respiratory rate 40/min, blood pressure 90/50 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 85%. She was pale, febrile, had non-exudative conjunctivitis, and gross hematuria.  
Initial laboratory tests showed normal electrolytes except for potassium 3.8 mEq/L, C-reactive protein >150 mg/L, lactate dehydrogenase 3570 U/L, fibrinogen 611 mg/dL, a negative direct and indirect Coombs test, and sufficient G6PD activity. Venous blood gas showed pH 7.43, PCO2 14.3 kPa, HCO3 9.6 mEq/L. Echocardiography was normal. Renal function was normal (BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.42 mg/dL). Liver enzymes were elevated (AST 2670 U/L, ALT 970 U/L, INR 1.76) with normal total bilirubin, direct bilirubin, and alkaline phosphatase. Urinalysis showed 4+ blood and 2+ albumin. Stool examination revealed 3+ occult blood and numerous white blood cells. Blood cultures showed no growth. Virology tests for HBV, HCV, HAV, SARS-CoV-2, and HSV 1 and 2 were negative.

A 63-year-old man with a 5-year history of poorly controlled hypertension was admitted 10 days ago for a spontaneous intracerebral hemorrhage managed nonoperatively. He was discharged after 3 days on analgesics and an anticonvulsant. He re-presented with 2 days of profound asthenia and dyspnea that acutely worsened on the day of admission. He also noted excessive polyuria over the preceding 3 days.

On examination, he was alert (GCS 15), hypotensive (74/30 mm Hg), tachycardic (135 bpm), mottled, and had cool extremities. He was tachypneic; cardiac and lung auscultation were unremarkable. He had dry mucous membranes and visible skin-fold dehydration. His weight was 65 kg (10 days earlier, 71 kg). Urine output was low (0.3 mL/kg/h).

ECG demonstrated sinus tachycardia. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a collapsible inferior vena cava, preserved LVEF (65%), and no tamponade. Arterial blood gas showed metabolic alkalosis and lactate of 3.64 mmol/L. Chest radiograph was normal.

After 30 mL/kg of saline, he remained hypotensive and required norepinephrine. Six hours later, blood pressure was 110/70 mm Hg, lactate had fallen to 1.8 mmol/L, and urine output improved.

Laboratory studies showed hemoconcentration, hyponatremia (123 mmol/L), hypochloremia (54 mEq/L), hypokalemia (2.3 mmol/L), low plasma osmolality (254 mmol/kg H₂O), elevated creatinine (120 μmol/L), and elevated urea (0.97 g/L), with normal inflammatory markers. Urine studies revealed natriuresis of 62 mmol/L and urine chloride of 64 mmol/L.

A 9-year-old girl was referred for an irregular heart rate noted at an incidental well-child visit. An electrocardiogram showed a prolonged QTc of 485 ms and runs of non-sustained bidirectional ventricular tachycardia. There was no family history of arrhythmogenic disorders, congenital heart disease, or sudden death; the mother and maternal grandmother had syndactyly. Physical examination revealed short stature, low-set ears, mild micrognathia, wide-spaced eyes, dental abnormalities, and left iris heterochromia. Vital signs were age-appropriate. Holter monitoring revealed a ventricular ectopy burden of 40%. Laboratory studies, including a CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, cardiac enzymes, and thyroid function tests, were normal. Exercise stress testing showed no significant increase in ectopy from baseline at higher heart rates. Transthoracic echocardiography and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated a structurally normal heart with no evidence of ventricular dysfunction.

A 16-year-old female presented with dissatisfaction with her smile due to badly broken upper front teeth. Clinical examination revealed enamel chipping and dentin exposure in teeth 11, 12, 21, 22, 34, and 44, accompanied by generalized enamel opacities. A digital orthopantomogram showed congenital absence of mandibular anterior teeth (31, 32, 33, 41, 42, and 43). Intraoral periapical radiographs of teeth 12, 11, 21, and 22 displayed radiolucency affecting enamel, dentin, and pulp, along with widened periodontal ligament spaces. Radiographs of teeth 34 and 44 revealed radiolucency in enamel and dentin without periapical changes. Pulp viability testing showed negative responses for teeth 12, 11, 21, and 22, and positive responses for teeth 34 and 44.

A 75-year-old woman with a history of coronary artery disease (balloon angioplasty and stenting of the left anterior descending and circumflex arteries 7 years earlier), hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes mellitus, and obesity presented with chest pain. An electrocardiogram during pain showed diffuse ST-segment depression. She received a loading dose of ticagrelor 180 mg and aspirin 325 mg and underwent urgent coronary angiography, which revealed chronic total occlusion of the right coronary artery and severe stenosis of the left main and circumflex arteries. Two drug-eluting stents were placed with excellent angiographic results. Postprocedural left ventricular ejection fraction was 50% with mild mitral regurgitation. A few hours later, telemetry showed Cheyne–Stokes respiration without overt heart failure, marked sinus arrhythmia with sinus tachycardia during apneic phases and severe sinus bradycardia (<30 beats/min) during hyperpneic phases, and cyclical transient complete atrioventricular block with pauses up to 5 seconds. Work-up including electrolytes, creatinine clearance, liver enzymes, and thyroid function was unrevealing.

A 24-year-old Indigenous Australian woman from a remote community presented with a 3-month history of left thigh swelling and pain associated with limitation of movement. She had type 1 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 14 years with chronically poor glycaemic control (HbA1c, 12.4% on admission; historically as high as 20.9%) and multiple microvascular complications, including proliferative retinopathy, nephrotic-range proteinuria, peripheral neuropathy, and autonomic neuropathy. Other history was notable for acute rheumatic fever, syphilis, recurrent urinary tract infections, active smoking (20 cigarettes per day), and prior right femoral neck fracture with internal fixation. Her only medication was premixed insulin (degludec and aspart 14 units twice daily).

On examination she was pale, cachectic (weight 32.5 kg; BMI 12.9 kg/m2), and sinus tachycardic at 120 bpm. She had significant tender swelling of the left thigh without erythema, warmth, or skin changes; peripheral pulses were intact. Neurologic assessment was limited by pain but revealed stocking-distribution sensory neuropathy and reduced proximal left lower-limb strength. Visual acuity was counting fingers bilaterally; funduscopic exam showed severe proliferative diabetic retinopathy with macular oedema.

Laboratory studies revealed normocytic anaemia (Hb 90 g/L), thrombocytosis (649 × 10^9/L), leukocytosis (20.3 × 10^9/L), elevated neutrophils (17.43 × 10^9/L), ESR 120 mm/h, CRP 185 mg/L, ferritin 1290 µg/L, and creatinine clearance 50 mL/min. Creatine kinase and liver function tests were normal. Thyroid function was normal; morning cortisol was 528 nmol/L. Nutritional studies showed hypoalbuminaemia (<15 g/L), vitamin D 10.3 nmol/L, and vitamin C 12 µmol/L; coeliac serology was negative. Urinalysis indicated a urinary tract infection and albumin/creatinine ratio 840 g/mol. Renal ultrasound demonstrated cystitis; renal biopsy confirmed class III diabetic nephropathy. 

CT of the pelvis and left leg showed ill-defined diffuse low attenuation in the left mid-thigh muscle measuring 36 × 34 × 150 mm without masses or collections. A Doppler ultrasound excluded deep vein thrombosis. MRI revealed marked oedema of the left anterior thigh musculature (rectus femoris, vastus medialis, vastus lateralis, and adductor muscles) with muscle atrophy, fatty and fluid replacement, and subcutaneous tissue oedema; the posterior compartment was spared.

A 35‐year‐old woman, 17 weeks’ gestation by last menstrual period, presented to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe abdominal pain that began 2 hours after defecating. She had confirmed pregnancy at 4 weeks’ gestation when she developed morning-sickness–type digestion discomfort. She reported no vaginal bleeding.

Her gynecologic history was significant for infrequent menstruation (4–5 days every 6 months), polycystic ovarian syndrome, left salpingectomy for tubal ectopic pregnancy 12 years ago, and seven in vitro fertilization–embryo transfer attempts; the seventh resulted in a pregnancy that ended in spontaneous abortion at 12 weeks, after which she discontinued fertility treatments and used no contraception.

On examination, her abdomen was rigid with rebound tenderness, especially on the left, where a tender mass appeared connected to the uterus. Initial laboratory results showed WBC 15.22 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 116 g/L, and serum amylase 41.9 IU/L. Transabdominal ultrasound revealed a viable 17-week fetus (fetal heart rate 155/min), a maximum vertical pocket of 6.8 cm, no free fluid, and a low-lying placenta with its edge near the internal cervical os.

Over the next 12 hours, her abdominal distention increased. Repeat labs showed WBC 13.23 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 98 g/L, and CRP 33.91 mg/L. On repeat ultrasound, the fetus’s heart rate was 163/min, but there was no detectable amniotic fluid, and a 2.0 cm low-echoic area was seen between the liver and kidney, consistent with new ascites.

An African woman in her early 40s presented with 4 years of gradually progressive visual loss, beginning in the right eye and then involving the left, associated with eye pain that worsened with movement and throbbing bitemporal headache. There was no fever, seizures, or altered mental status. She was initially diagnosed with idiopathic intracranial hypertension despite a normal brain MRI and normal CSF opening pressure, and underwent ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement after acetazolamide failed.

One year after the shunt, she developed numbness in her lower and upper extremities. She noted progressive difficulty standing from sitting, climbing stairs, sitting unsupported, handling objects, writing, buttoning shirts, and combing hair. She also developed bladder and bowel incontinence. There was no history of joint pain, weight loss, skin changes, or preceding respiratory or gastrointestinal illness.

Her medical history included 6 years of hypertension, well controlled on medications. Obstetric history was notable for 11 spontaneous pregnancy losses before 24 weeks and one living child; menstrual history was otherwise unremarkable. There was no history of preeclampsia, thrombosis, or similar family illnesses.

On examination, she was alert with normal cognition and no meningeal signs. Cranial nerves were intact except for the optic nerves. Ocular motility was full; visual acuity was light perception only in both eyes, intraocular pressures were 17 mmHg (right) and 18 mmHg (left), pupils were dilated and nonreactive, and fundoscopy showed bilateral optic disc pallor with peripapillary atrophy and otherwise normal retinal background. Motor examination revealed normal bulk, strength 2/5 in upper limbs and 1/5 in lower limbs, brisk deep tendon reflexes, and bilateral extensor plantar responses. Sensory examination showed decreased pinprick, fine touch, temperature, and vibration below the C4 level; spine palpation was unremarkable. Other systems were normal.

A 62-year-old Indian man with recently diagnosed p16-negative posterior oropharyngeal wall squamous cell carcinoma (stage IVb, T3N3bM0) and comorbid hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and ischemic heart failure (ejection fraction 23% with an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator) underwent elective feeding gastrostomy. His thyroid function was normal at initial cancer staging. Three weeks later, he developed fever, tachycardia, and stridor. Flexible nasolaryngoscopy showed near-complete oropharyngeal obstruction; an emergency tracheostomy was performed. Examination under anesthesia revealed rapid progression of the tumour into a circumferential oropharyngeal stricture. Despite 17 days of piperacillin-tazobactam, his fever persisted (maximum 39.6 °C) and heart rate remained 110–120 bpm. Extensive cultures (blood, urine, sputum, gastrostomy site), HIV and cryptococcal tests, echocardiography, and brain CT were negative. Laboratory studies showed C-reactive protein 243.3 g/L and a white-cell count of 18,400/mm3 (75.1% neutrophils). Chest radiography demonstrated a small right pleural effusion. Neck CT revealed worsening pharyngeal disease with invasion into bilateral thyroid lobes and new bilateral lung nodules. On examination, there was central neck tenderness without a palpable goiter. Thyroid function tests showed triiodothyronine 8.0 pmol/L (reference 3.5–6.0), free thyroxine 54 pmol/L (reference 8.0–16.0), and thyroid-stimulating hormone 0.01 mIU/L (reference 0.45–4.5). Antithyroid peroxidase, thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor, and thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin antibodies were negative. Thyroid ultrasonography showed reduced vascularity without abscess formation.
differential_diagnoses: Thyroid hormone excess from exogenous source

case_text: A 45-year-old woman presented with a 3-day history of malaise and upper respiratory symptoms for which she had taken ibuprofen and pseudoephedrine. On initial assessment she was febrile (38.5 °C), hypotensive, and tachycardic with a heart rate of 250 bpm. Vagal maneuvers and escalating doses of intravenous adenosine did not affect her rhythm. At the community hospital her ECG showed a narrow QRS tachycardia up to 280 bpm. She received several liters of crystalloid and an infusion of IV diltiazem without sustained slowing, and six attempts at electrical cardioversion under sedation failed to terminate the tachycardia. Over the next hours, control was attempted with IV esmolol, digoxin, magnesium sulfate, and a 150 mg loading dose of amiodarone. As she developed increasing oxygen requirements and pulmonary edema, she was given IV furosemide and transferred to the cardiac ICU at a tertiary center. There she received a second amiodarone load, three more unsuccessful cardioversions, and boluses of lidocaine and procainamide with only transient rate slowing. With worsening respiratory status she was intubated, cooled to 35.5 °C, and placed on infusions of esmolol, lidocaine, and procainamide. Her heart rate slowed to 130 bpm and ECG demonstrated atrioventricular dissociation.

A 45-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, presented at 37+4 weeks’ gestation after an obstetric ultrasound suggested a teratoma. The initial ultrasound at 37+3 weeks showed a fetal-like echo in the thoracic cavity of the fetus. On repeat examination, there was a live singleton fetus in cephalic presentation with a right retroperitoneal mass of mixed echogenicity. Two parallel fetal-like signals were seen within the mass: one exhibited a weak heartbeat, and the other was acardiac.  
Both parents were healthy, there was no history of twin gestation, and the first child had no congenital abnormalities.  
Ultrasonography revealed:  
• A 4.7 × 2.1 cm cystic-solid mass in the right retroperitoneum with intra-cyst septation and mixed echogenicity adherent to the cyst wall; no head, spine, upper limb, or heart echoes in this mass.  
• Continuous scanning showed one limb with skin covering, a long bone, distal footpad, and toe-like echoes. An umbilical cord–like vascular echo was connected to the mass, but color Doppler showed no blood flow.  
• A second 3.9 × 2.1 cm irregular mass posteriorly, lacking head, spine, or upper limbs; sagittal sections revealed a buttock-like contour, a limb with a proximal long bone and plantar echo, all covered with skin. A faint fetal heartbeat was detected in this mass; color Doppler demonstrated blood flow, and pulsed-wave Doppler showed a two-way arterial spectrum. Continuous fetal movements were observed.  
• The masses compressed the host fetus’s inferior vena cava and abdominal aorta, causing arch-shaped shifting; the intestines were displaced leftward, and the right kidney was displaced into the pelvis. Renal blood flow to the live mass derived from the host fetus’s abdominal aorta.

A 71-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of persistent headache. Seven months earlier, she underwent right frontal craniotomy for a transitional (WHO Grade I) falx meningioma, which was completely excised (Simpson Grade I). Preoperatively, she received antihistamines and corticosteroids for poliallergic asthma. Hemostasis was achieved by filling the surgical cavity with absorbable hemostatic agents (bovine-derived gelatine with human-derived thrombin matrix and oxidized regenerated cellulose), and a dural graft was placed. Her postoperative course was uneventful. Early CT and MRI scans confirmed complete tumor removal. Blood tests showed elevated high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (72.9 mg/L; normal <5 mg/L) but no fever, leukocytosis, or procalcitonin elevation. She was discharged on schedule.

On presentation, the surgical wound was fully healed. There was no fever, and hs-CRP remained slightly elevated (59.4 mg/L). Lumbar puncture yielded normal CSF both chemically and microbiologically. Noncontrast CT of the brain showed a 2.5 cm irregular, contrast-enhancing lesion along the right frontal falx, with surrounding edema and mass effect. MRI demonstrated that the lesion was isointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted, diffusion-weighted, and FLAIR sequences. A relapse of meningioma was suspected, and surgical re-exploration was planned.

An 81-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of a 1-month history of progressive dyspnea. She had smoked 30 pack-years. Three months earlier, she had been diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis and started on bucillamine (200 mg/day) and prednisolone (4 mg/day), with improvement in her arthritis symptoms. Before starting bucillamine, fine crackles were noted over both lower lung fields, and a chest radiograph showed reticular shadows in the bilateral lower lung zones. Her medical history included angina pectoris and mitral valve replacement one year earlier. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis.

On examination, she was afebrile, with a respiratory rate of 24 breaths per minute. Fine crackles were audible bilaterally; there were no signs of congestive heart failure. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (10,900/µL) with neutrophilia (81.5%), elevated lactate dehydrogenase (408 U/L), C-reactive protein (5.51 mg/dL), Krebs von den Lungen-6 (2,140 U/mL), surfactant protein-D (299.0 ng/mL), rheumatoid factor (352 IU/mL), anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody (≥1,200 U/mL), and hypoxemia (PaO2 87.2 mmHg on 3 L/min nasal oxygen). A lymphocyte stimulation test for bucillamine was negative (stimulation index, 168%). Brain natriuretic peptide, immunoglobulins, β-D-glucan, and other autoantibodies were normal or negative.

Pulmonary function tests revealed a vital capacity of 1.11 L (53.9% predicted), forced vital capacity of 1.08 L (52.4% predicted), FEV1 of 1.03 L (77.4% predicted), and DLCO of 2.23 mL/min/mmHg (17.7% predicted). Chest radiography demonstrated infiltrative opacities with reticular shadows in the bilateral middle and lower lung fields. High-resolution CT showed ground-glass opacities and consolidations in both lungs, along with reticular opacities, honeycombing, and traction bronchiectasis in the lower lobes, consistent with pre-existing usual interstitial pneumonia; note that ground-glass opacities and consolidations spared the lung bases. Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid contained 2.45×10^5 cells/mL (44.0% macrophages, 25.5% lymphocytes, 26.0% neutrophils, 4.5% eosinophils; CD4/CD8 ratio 0.2), and cultures were negative. Transbronchial lung biopsy revealed alveolar organizing pneumonia, septal thickening, and inflammatory cell infiltration.

A 61-year-old African-American man presented with a four-day history of binocular diplopia, left eyelid droop, and numbness and tingling in both hands and feet. He also reported one month of profound fatigue and progressive proximal weakness, to the point of being unable to ambulate. His medical history was notable for biopsy-proven sarcoidosis treated with steroids in 1970, gout, dyspepsia, and mild obstructive pulmonary disease. He had lost 30 pounds over six months and had a chronic dry cough.

On examination, vital signs were normal. Cranial-nerve testing revealed significant left eyelid ptosis (1 mm aperture), impaired adduction, abduction, and upward gaze bilaterally, partial right III and VI nerve palsies, and almost complete left III and VI nerve palsies. There was subtle bilateral facial weakness, decreased left facial sensation to pinprick, and proximal muscle atrophy with 4/5 strength in the shoulders and hips, 3/5 in lower extremities, preserved distal strength, decreased to absent reflexes bilaterally, and mildly decreased distal leg sensation. The abdomen was diffusely mildly tender; joints were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL, normal calcium, electrolytes, renal function, HIV serology, vitamin B12, folate, muscle enzymes, and infectious serologies except mildly elevated AST and total bilirubin. ESR was 66 mm/hr, CRP normal, and urinalysis revealed 3+ proteinuria (normal six months earlier). Head CT was normal.

He was treated with five days of IVIG for a presumed Guillain-Barré–like syndrome. Chest CT showed multiple calcified nodules (some larger than prior studies) and a new right lower lobe infiltrate. Brain MRI/MRA (including orbits with gadolinium) was unremarkable. Nerve conduction studies and EMG revealed a diffuse, predominantly axonal, length-dependent sensory-motor polyneuropathy with myopathic features. Lumbar MRI showed multilevel degeneration. CSF analysis demonstrated albuminocytologic dissociation (protein 105 mg/dL). CT abdomen and pelvis showed minimal free fluid. Fine-needle aspiration of the lung infiltrate showed inflammatory cells and plasma cells without granulomas; cultures were negative. Muscle biopsy showed inflammatory myopathy with fiber atrophy and regeneration without vasculitis. Sural nerve biopsy revealed no acute abnormality but evidence of possible old vasculitis.

A 53-year-old man with hypertension presented with five days of intractable vomiting, generalized weakness, ataxia, and severe headaches. On examination, he was lethargic, retching frequently, and had bilateral papilledema with impaired upward and downward gaze; gait could not be assessed safely. CT and MRI of the brain demonstrated hydrocephalus from obstruction of the third ventricle by a 2.5 × 3.0 × 3.2 cm hyperdense, contrast-enhancing mass centered in the pineal region with central calcification and mass effect on an edematous midbrain. 

He underwent endoscopic biopsy with third ventriculostomy and external ventricular drainage, followed by a subtotal resection via a supracerebellar infratentorial craniotomy. Histologic examination showed a highly cellular neoplasm of primitive round cells with finely dispersed chromatin, scant cytoplasm, brisk mitotic activity (20 per 10 high-power fields), focal papillary arrangements, and vague rosettes. Immunohistochemistry revealed strong positivity for synaptophysin, neuron-specific enolase, cytokeratin 8/18, CK AE1/AE3, and CAM 5.2; focal positivity for chromogranin, S100, and neurofilament; negativity for CK7, CK20, epithelial membrane antigen, thyroid transcription factor-1, glial fibrillary acidic protein, and parathyroid hormone; intact INI1; and a Ki-67 index of 60–70%. 

A PET/CT scan showed FDG avidity solely in the pineal region. A small, non–FDG-avid lesion in the left renal upper pole was noted incidentally.

A 57-year-old left-handed chef presented with a 5-year history of progressive weakness and decreased function in his left hand. Initial symptoms were mild numbness of the middle finger, treated conservatively as suspected carpal tunnel syndrome. Over several years the numbness progressed to the thumb and index finger, accompanied by grip and pinch weakness. Referral was prompted when he developed increasing forearm pain and complete loss of strength in thumb and index flexion, rendering him unable to work. He denied nocturnal symptoms and had no history of heavy lifting.

Medical history included smoking and left-sided male breast cancer treated by mastectomy, axillary node clearance, radiotherapy, and ongoing tamoxifen.

On examination, there was marked wasting of the thenar eminence and subtle forearm muscle atrophy. Sensation was reduced in the thumb, index, and middle fingers, and in the thenar triangle. Motor testing revealed 0/5 strength in flexor pollicis longus, flexor digitorum profundus, and flexor digitorum superficialis of the index finger, with failure to form an “O-sign.” A Tinel sign was elicited 12 cm distal to the medial epicondyle. All provocative tests for carpal tunnel syndrome were negative.

Bilateral humeral radiographs showed no supracondylar processes, and none were palpable. Neurophysiology studies demonstrated no recordable median nerve motor responses on the left, with preserved sensory responses at the wrist. There was no evidence of widespread brachial plexus pathology or carpal tunnel syndrome, and findings indicated a severe left median mononeuropathy with acute and chronic denervation.

A 23-year-old Sri Lankan female law student presented with multiple episodes of nonbilious, nonprojectile vomiting over two days, associated with burning chest pain and regurgitation. She reported a 3-year history of similar cyclical vomiting episodes—each lasting 4–5 days with 4–6 daily episodes—followed by symptom-free intervals. These episodes were managed as gastroesophageal reflux disease with proton-pump inhibitors and prokinetics without full investigations. She denied abdominal pain, fever, weight loss, or bulimic behaviors; there were no signs of an eating disorder. She had reduced appetite, generalized weakness for 3 years, and secondary amenorrhea since age 20. She had psychosocial stressors, including a recent breakup. On exam, BMI was 16 kg/m^2, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, pulse 78 beats/min, and the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed:  
- Serum sodium 110 mmol/L, potassium 3.2 mmol/L, chloride 74 mmol/L (repeatedly low)  
- Serum osmolality 223 mOsm/kg; urine osmolality 266 mOsm/kg; urine Na+ 14 mmol/L  
- Random serum cortisol 105 nmol/L (measured at 1 a.m.)  
- TSH 0.209 µIU/mL, free T3 1.32 pg/mL, free T4 0.98 ng/dL  
- FSH 1.4 mIU/mL, LH 0.4 mIU/mL  
- Serum prolactin 90.8 ng/mL after dilution  

She was euvolemic, denied polydipsia or polyuria, and had a normal urine output.

A 54-year-old man presented with progressively worsening shortness of breath over 1 month, on a background of exertional dyspnea for 1 year and increasing fatigue. Recently, he developed orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and progressive bilateral leg swelling. He was treated with furosemide 40 mg twice daily and enalapril 5 mg twice daily with no improvement; enalapril caused dizziness and worsened fatigue. He has no personal or family history of hypertension or diabetes and denies syncope, neurologic symptoms, joint pain, or autonomic symptoms.

On examination, blood pressure was 100/60 mm Hg, pulse 110 bpm, respiratory rate 23 /min. Lung auscultation revealed bibasilar dullness and decreased air entry, more on the right. The jugular venous pressure was elevated; the precordium was quiet, and there was a systolic murmur at the left sternal border. The liver was enlarged 6 cm below the costal margin and tender, with shifting dullness; there was grade ++ bilateral pitting edema.

Laboratory tests showed normal renal function, electrolytes, liver function, and urinalysis. Hemoglobin was 11.7 g/dL (MCV 91, MCH 29). Serum troponin was elevated at 656 ng/L and 578 ng/L (normal < 100 ng/L). Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated hepatic congestion with hepatomegaly, ascites, and bilateral pleural effusions. Chest X-ray confirmed a right pleural effusion. ECG showed low-voltage limb leads with a pseudoinfarction pattern in precordial leads and ST-segment depression with T-wave inversions in V4–V6.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed biatrial enlargement and biventricular hypertrophy (IVS 22 mm, LVPW 21 mm, RV wall 14 mm) with interatrial septal thickening and granular “sparkling” myocardium. Left ventricular ejection fraction was 65% with no regional wall motion abnormalities. Doppler indices showed a restrictive filling pattern (E/A ratio 11, septal e′ 3, E/e′ 18) and atrial arrest. Mitral, aortic, and tricuspid valves were thickened; the IVC was plethoric, and there was minimal pericardial effusion.

A 16-year-old girl presented with a 7-day history of diffuse abdominal tenderness. She had no chronic illnesses, no alcohol or drug use, and no known tuberculosis exposures. Vital signs were normal. On examination, she had moderate ascites without other abnormalities.

Laboratory studies showed: 
- Normal blood counts and chemistries  
- CRP 39 mg/L (0–5)  
- ESR 42 mm/h (0–20)  

Chest radiograph was normal. Abdominal ultrasound revealed free fluid with septations, multiple small nodular implants on peritoneal surfaces (largest 16 mm), and a septated left ovarian cyst measuring 39 × 27 mm. Paracentesis yielded lymphocyte-predominant fluid without malignant cells, with a serum–ascites albumin gradient <1.1 g/dL.

Thyroid function tests were normal. Serum tumor markers were: 
- AFP 2.48 ng/mL (0–7)  
- CEA 0.39 ng/mL (0–3.8)  
- CA15-3 26.15 U/mL (0–26.4)  
- CA19-9 1.14 U/mL (0–27)  
- CA125 107.5 U/mL (0–35)  

Brucella and viral serologies were negative. Tuberculin skin testing was anergic, and QuantiFERON PCR and acid-fast staining of ascitic fluid were negative.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck, chest, and abdomen showed bilateral cervical, supraclavicular, paratracheal, subcarinal, anterior diaphragmatic, and cardiophrenic lymphadenopathy; nasopharyngeal soft-tissue thickening; omental nodularity with peritoneal implants; ascites; a 23 × 18 mm left ovarian cyst; and mesenteric lymph nodes. Ascitic fluid adenosine deaminase was elevated at 62 U/L (0–30). Abdominal MRI confirmed bilateral ovarian cysts, dilated fallopian tubes, nodular peritoneal opacities, and moderate ascites.

An 18-year-old male was admitted with psychomotor excitement, polyuria, polydipsia, vomiting, and a seven-month history of progressive right-sided hemiparesis with dystonia. He had two years of poor school performance, anxiety, and emotional lability. Seven months earlier, a nonenhanced head CT was normal.

On examination, he appeared pale with a BMI of 14.4 kg/m2 and blood pressure of 90/60 mmHg with a poor response to fluids. Sexual maturation was Tanner stage 3 with small testes (6 mL) and pubic and axillary hair stage G2. Neurologically, he had right hemiparesis with hyperreflexia and dystonia. Laboratory studies showed polyuria with low urine osmolality, hypernatremia, hypotension refractory to fluid resuscitation, and panhypopituitarism, leading to treatment with hydrocortisone followed by levothyroxine and desmopressin.

Brain MRI revealed a solid, homogeneously enhancing mass in the pituitary and hypothalamus with marked pituitary stalk thickening and absence of the posterior pituitary bright spot. There was T2 hyperintensity in the left internal capsule, corona radiata, caudate nucleus, and bilateral globus pallidus, with mild atrophy of the left internal capsule and ipsilateral cerebral peduncle. Differential diagnoses included nonadenomatous lesions of the pituitary stalk and ectopic germinoma. Chest CT and serum angiotensin-converting enzyme levels were normal. Skull and long-bone radiographs showed no lytic lesions. CSF examination revealed no atypical cells; CSF α-fetoprotein was negative and CSF β-hCG was slightly elevated.

A 7-month-old intact female Maine Coon kitten was evaluated for a 2-month history of severe regurgitation occurring seconds to minutes after eating or drinking, with episodes ranging from twice weekly to twice daily. Weight loss was noted over this period despite a normal appetite, water consumption, and activity level. Previous treatments—including maropitant, cimetidine, amoxicillin/clavulanic acid, and gastrointestinal diets—had no effect. The kitten had a history of chronic vomiting of undigested food and bile from age 3–5 months and a self-resolving episode of acute vomiting at 5 months. No regurgitation or vomiting occurred before 3 months of age. On presentation, the kitten was bright, alert, weighed 2.5 kg with a body condition score of 2/9, and had stunted growth; the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. Thoracic radiographs obtained before and after oral contrast demonstrated severe and diffuse oesophageal dilatation consistent with generalized megaoesophagus, with persistent contrast and aeric distension of the oesophageal lumen and apparent loss of continuity between the distal oesophagus and the stomach. No aspiration pneumonia was evident.

A 64-year-old man with rheumatoid arthritis (RF positive, anti-CCP negative), diagnosed in 2003, initially treated with methotrexate and low-dose prednisolone, presented in early 2007 with an RA flare treated successfully with prednisolone and leflunomide. Six months later, despite no joint pain or ultrasonographic evidence of synovitis, he reported fatigue, night sweats, a 10-kg weight gain, and persistent elevation of C-reactive protein (CRP). He then developed paresthesia and pressure pain in both lower legs; extensive evaluation—including echocardiography, sonography of joints, soft tissues, and abdomen; CT of the thorax; repeated blood cultures; bone marrow aspiration; and urine testing—failed to identify any infectious or malignant cause. Serologic testing showed normal ANA, ANCA, anti–dsDNA, complement C3/C4, anti-CCP, and anti-MCV levels; RF remained positive at baseline levels. A positron emission tomography scan demonstrated focal FDG uptake in the lower limbs without a vasculitic pattern. Treatment was switched to sulfasalazine and high-dose prednisolone with temporary CRP improvement, but one month later he returned with high fevers (up to 40 °C), CRP 220 mg/L, night sweats, and multiple 3–4 cm painful nodules on the inner thighs causing immobility. There were still no signs of synovitis. MRI of the lower limbs showed acute fasciitis. A painful nodule was excised, and histologic examination revealed lobular panniculitis with a mixed inflammatory infiltrate dominated by neutrophils.

A 55-year-old man presented with lower urinary tract symptoms characterized by hesitancy, poor urinary stream, and urgency. He denied flank pain, colicky pain, haematuria, weight loss, or constitutional symptoms. His medical history was unremarkable, and he had no prior genitourinary disease. He was afebrile, with stable vital signs, and a physical examination was normal.

Laboratory studies showed a serum prostate-specific antigen level of 5.1 ng/ml and a serum creatinine concentration of 86 μmol/l. Urine microscopy demonstrated no haematuria or atypical cells. Transrectal ultrasound–guided prostate biopsies revealed benign prostatic hyperplasia.

An abdominal ultrasound performed to evaluate his lower urinary tract symptoms incidentally revealed an exophytic lesion in the right kidney measuring approximately 3.0 × 3.2 × 4.2 cm. The lesion was homogeneous and hypoechoic with a well-defined margin, located at the lateral border of the ventral mid-zone of the kidney. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the abdomen confirmed a soft-tissue mass in the same location, measuring up to 2.17 cm in maximum diameter, with a small area of calcification; the lesion was exophytic and situated away from the renal hilum.
differential_diagnoses: Metastatic renal cell carcinoma to the kidney
Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1) associated with parathyroid adenoma
Renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid features

case_text:
A 49-year-old amateur athlete presented with 3 weeks of exercise intolerance manifesting as exertional dyspnoea and dizziness. He denied syncope, had no family history of sudden cardiac death, and took no medications. He had traveled to Iran and, 3 months earlier, had an inguinal bullous skin lesion treated briefly with topical steroids without recurrence.

On examination: heart rate 61 bpm, blood pressure 122/77 mmHg, temperature 36.5 °C; cardiac and pulmonary exams were normal. Laboratory tests showed normal leukocytes (4.89 g/L) and CRP (0.8 mg/L). Resting ECG revealed an incomplete right bundle branch block and first-degree AV block; rhythm monitoring showed intermittent 2:1 AV block. During spiroergometry, exercise-induced high-degree AV block was documented.

Transthoracic echocardiography raised suspicion of inferobasal septal wall-motion abnormalities and, retrospectively, a mass; left ventricular ejection fraction was normal. Coronary angiography excluded coronary artery disease, and chest CT revealed no pulmonary pathology. Cardiac MRI demonstrated a 5 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm mass in the inferobasal septum with late gadolinium enhancement but no active perfusion. FDG-PET showed intense uptake in the inferobasal septum and no extracardiac uptake.

Further evaluation revealed normal sIL-2 receptor, ANA, ANCA, serum calcium, and ACE levels. Blood cultures (aerobic, anaerobic, mycobacterial) and serologies for Brucella, Bartonella henselae, Treponema pallidum, Borrelia burgdorferi, Coxiella burnetii, and HIV were negative. PCR for tuberculosis and mycobacterial cultures were negative. Dermatologic full-body exam showed only non-specific skin changes.

Because there were no extracardiac manifestations, transthoracic and transesophageal echocardiography-guided endomyocardial biopsies were performed, but all samples were non-specific.

A 47-year-old previously healthy woman presented with four months of generalized body swelling, disproportionate ascites, loss of appetite, and weight loss. She denied fever, night sweats, jaundice, bleeding, cough, or TB exposure. There was no history of cardiac, renal, or liver disease; no alcohol use; and an HIV test was negative. On exam she was emaciated (BMI 18), afebrile, with moderate ascites, mild ankle edema, no stigmata of chronic liver disease, and no hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies showed normochromic normocytic anemia, thrombocytosis, normal liver enzymes except mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase and GGT, normal INR, normal renal function, and a serum CA-125 of 175 U/ml (normal <46). Chest radiograph was normal; echocardiography showed normal cardiac structure and function without constrictive pericarditis. Transvaginal ultrasound revealed no ovarian pathology. Abdominal ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT suggested heterogeneous liver parenchyma, minimal surface nodularity, and moderate ascites without portal vein thrombosis or lymphadenopathy, raising concern for decompensated chronic liver disease. Workup for cirrhosis was unrevealing, and upper endoscopy showed no varices or portal gastropathy. Diuretics were started. Diagnostic paracentesis revealed exudative ascites with elevated inflammatory markers; cultures (bacterial, fungal), acid-fast bacilli (AFB) smear, TB culture, and GeneXpert on fluid were negative, and cytology was negative for malignant cells. The tuberculin skin test measured 17 mm induration.

A 63-year-old man with a history of duodenal ulcer repair and mesh insertion for an abdominal incisional hernia was admitted with fever and found to have pus in his abdomen on examination. He had no history of thrombotic events, smoking, drug use, or family history of coagulopathy. Laboratory tests showed a C-reactive protein level of 2.63 mg/dL, fibrinogen 493 mg/dL, and a normal antithrombin III level; blood cultures were negative. He was diagnosed with a mesh infection and received 4 weeks of antibiotic therapy before transfer for abdominal surgical debridement. Preoperative transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 39 mm mass in the left atrium adherent to the atrial septum, an ejection fraction of 56%, and no valvular abnormalities. Contrast-enhanced CT also demonstrated a 39 mm left atrial mass with partial calcification. Brain MRI showed minuscule, scattered cerebral infarctions. Based on these findings, the mass was presumed to be a myxoma and surgical resection was scheduled.

A 13-year-old boy with mixed gonadal dysgenesis and a two-stage proximal hypospadias repair as an infant presented with 2 days of lower abdominal pain and dysuria. He had occasional vomiting, loose stools, and had had a fever 1 day earlier. In the ED, he was afebrile, with mild lower abdominal distension and suprapubic tenderness. Urinalysis showed turbid urine with too numerous to count white blood cells; a urine culture was sent. He was discharged on oral cephalexin for presumed UTI.

He returned 2 days later with persistent end-of-void suprapubic and right lower quadrant pain, dysuria, urgency, frequency, and foul-smelling urine. He denied fevers or chills; diarrhea had resolved. He reported decreased appetite and small, clumped stools but denied chronic constipation. His mother noted a small amount of urethral discharge. On exam, he was afebrile, with lower abdominal tenderness; genitourinary exam revealed a circumcised penis, an absent left testis, and a mildly tender right testis with normal lie and reflexes. Repeat urinalysis again showed too numerous to count white blood cells, though the previous urine culture had no growth. Peripheral white count was normal and CRP was 20 mg/dL.

Scrotal ultrasound showed a normal right testicle with normal flow. Abdominal ultrasound did not fully visualize the appendix and showed no secondary signs of appendicitis. Retroperitoneal ultrasound revealed normal kidneys and bladder but reported “circumferential wall thickening of the rectum with an air-fluid level.” The differential diagnosis included UTI, appendicitis, scrotal pathology, severe constipation, or gastroenteritis.

A 15-year-old spayed female domestic shorthair cat was examined for a routine checkup. Physical examination, complete blood count, and serum biochemistry were unremarkable. Abdominal ultrasound revealed a 9 × 7 mm heterogeneous hypoechoic nodule at the periphery of the quadrate liver lobe. Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration cytology showed numerous grouped epithelial cells with moderate to marked anisocytosis and anisokaryosis, round nuclei with reticular to coarse chromatin, prominent nucleoli, and poorly basophilic cytoplasm. A presumptive diagnosis of epithelial neoplasia (carcinoma) was made, and metastatic disease was considered. Serial monitoring showed progressive growth of the nodule to 1.73 × 1.34 cm over 4 months, with a transient rise in AST and ALT. Laparoscopic liver biopsy was then performed; histopathology suggested lymphocytic cholangitis, and symptomatic treatment normalized transaminases, but the nodule continued to enlarge. Whole-body CT showed a diffuse heterogeneous liver parenchyma, especially in the quadrate lobe, without any other abnormalities. A partial lobectomy of the quadrate lobe was performed. Histopathology of the excised mass revealed multilobulated epithelial cells forming follicular structures containing dense eosinophilic, colloid-like material, merging into tubular and solid proliferations with nuclear crowding and mitotic figures. Immunohistochemistry showed diffuse cytoplasmic positivity for thyroglobulin in most neoplastic cells. Thyroxine (T4) and thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) concentrations were within reference intervals. Re-evaluation of the CT images revealed no abnormalities in the orthotopic thyroid gland and no nodular lesions elsewhere.

A 72-year-old man presented with a 3-year history of progressive anorexia, general malaise, and a 15 kg weight loss that left him unable to walk. His medical history was notable only for intestinal volvulus surgery at age 14 years. On admission, he was alert; temperature was 36.9 °C, blood pressure 124/86 mmHg, pulse 60 beats/min. He appeared cachectic (BMI 16.4 kg/m2). Neurologic, respiratory, cardiac, and abdominal examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed:
- Hypoalbuminemia (2.1 g/dL) and low total protein (4.9 g/dL)
- Elevated C-reactive protein (2.75 mg/dL)
- Elevated soluble interleukin-2 receptor (1,950 U/mL) and interleukin-6 (20.7 pg/mL)
- Hemoglobin 6.6 g/dL; other values notable for normal LFTs and renal function.

Abdominal CT demonstrated thickening of the small-intestinal mucosa and intra-abdominal lymphadenopathy. Upper endoscopy revealed yellow-white, shaggy mucosa in the duodenum; biopsy specimens showed no specific abnormalities. The patient received intravenous hyperalimentation without improvement. Given the high sIL-2R and IL-6 levels and lymphadenopathy, oral prednisolone (25 mg daily) was initiated, leading to improved appetite and weight gain. He was discharged on day 33. One month later, he lost his appetite again and was readmitted for further evaluation.

A 26-year-old man presented with two weeks of progressive lower-limb edema and exertional dyspnea. He had chronic sinusitis and a family history of celiac disease. Two months earlier, he had eaten raw fish without gastrointestinal symptoms. On examination, he was afebrile and hemodynamically stable, with muffled heart sounds and pitting edema of the legs.

Laboratory tests showed eosinophilia (980/mm^3; normal 0–700), elevated total IgE, and C-reactive protein of 12 mg/dL (normal <5). ECG was normal. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a large pericardial effusion with “swinging heart,” ventricular interdependence, a dilated inferior vena cava, respiratory variation of mitral inflow of 25%, and an LVEF of 48%. Contrast CT of the chest and abdomen showed pericardial and pleural effusions and mediastinal lymphadenopathy up to 15 mm. 

He was started on colchicine and ibuprofen. Echo-guided pericardiocentesis drained 1150 mL of serous fluid. Fluid cultures were negative and cytology revealed normal lymphocytes, macrophages, and mesothelial cells, without eosinophils. His LVEF normalized and edema improved, but eosinophilia persisted (940–1100/mm^3) and a residual posterior effusion of 16 mm remained.

Cardiac MRI demonstrated pericardial and subepicardial late gadolinium enhancement and myocardial edema on T2 mapping, with an LVEF of 54%. PET-FDG scan showed intense uptake in the pericardium and mediastinal lymph nodes. 

Extensive testing was performed: serologies for viral and bacterial causes of pericarditis were negative; inhalant allergy tests and skin prick tests were negative; stool studies were normal; serologies for Toxocara canis, Strongyloides stercoralis, Ascaris lumbricoides, and Trichinella spiralis were negative. Echinococcus granulosus IgG was mildly elevated at 1:64 (positive >1:32), and Anisakis simplex IgE was 0.33 kUA/L (positive >0.10). Peripheral-blood and bone-marrow immunophenotyping and cytology showed expansion of normal eosinophils without malignancy. Autoantibodies for eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis were negative, and sinus imaging was unremarkable.

A 14-year-old girl presented with a 3-week history of bilateral mandibular swelling and morning purulent oral discharge. She was otherwise well, with stable vital signs. On examination, there was bilateral facial swelling in the mandibular region, multiple dark brown nevi on the palms and soles (unchanged in size for ≥5 years), and clinical macrocephaly. Intraorally, there was bilateral buccal cortical expansion in the posterior mandible with moderate pus discharge adjacent to tooth 36; teeth were firm and without paresthesia.

Orthopantomogram showed expansile radiolucent lesions in the mandible bilaterally, extending from the first molar region to the coronoid processes, with impacted second and third molars displaced into the coronoid areas and cortical thinning and erosion at multiple sites. A smaller radiolucent lesion with an impacted third molar was seen in the maxilla. 

Incisional biopsy from multiple sites revealed a parakeratinized stratified squamous epithelial lining, six to eight cell layers thick, with surface corrugation and basal cell palisading, and a moderately collagenous fibrous capsule. The mandibular lesions were initially marsupialized; three months later, enucleation specimens showed similar histology plus budding of basal epithelial cells, keratin-filled daughter cysts, odontogenic epithelial islands, and focal crowding with mild atypia of basal and parabasal layers.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the head demonstrated calcifications within the falx cerebri. A chest radiograph was normal.

A 44-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of a 5-year history of persistent headaches and nausea. Over this period, she noted progressively worsening vision in both eyes and short-term memory loss. She was born in Honduras and continues to travel there regularly; she has no history of fever, night sweats, weight loss, rash, oral or genital ulcers, or sick contacts. Her past medical history is unremarkable; she does not smoke and has no pets.

On examination, her vital signs were within normal limits. Neurologic examination revealed decreased peripheral vision in both eyes; the remainder of the neurologic and general physical examination was normal. Formal ophthalmologic evaluation showed no signs of ocular cysticercosis.

Initial laboratory studies showed:  
• White blood cell count 7000 cells/mcL  
• Hemoglobin 12 g/dL  
• Platelets 333,000 cells/mcL  
• Creatinine 1 mg/dL  
• Glucose 109 mg/dL  
• Aspartate aminotransferase 111 units/L  
• Alanine aminotransferase 149 units/L  
• Morning cortisol 1.1 mcg/dL (low)  
• Adrenocorticotropic hormone 5.8 pg/mL (low)  

Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain revealed a partially calcified suprasellar and parasellar mass with both solid and cystic components, the largest segment measuring approximately 1.0 × 1.3 cm in the right parasellar region.

A 41-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of a progressively enlarging oral lesion over 8 months. She had no significant medical history but recalled an oral surgery at the same site 8 years earlier (records unavailable). Intraoral examination revealed a well-defined, lobulated nodule measuring 3.0 cm, covered by normal mucosa, extending from the palatal gingiva of the right first and second molars onto the hard palate. The lesion was firm on palpation, painless, and slightly mobile. A removable partial prosthesis had caused mild trauma to the adjacent premolar region. Panoramic radiography was normal. An incisional biopsy was performed; the specimen was rubbery, soft to moderately firm, and gray-white in color.

A 33-year-old primigravida at 12 weeks’ gestation presented with right flank pain radiating to the right lower quadrant, nausea, and vomiting. She had a history of hypertension, previously controlled on lisinopril, switched to labetalol 200 mg daily during pregnancy, and took aspirin 81 mg daily. On presentation, she was afebrile; white blood cell count was 12 × 10^3/mL, platelets were normal, and urinalysis confirmed a urinary tract infection. Abdominal ultrasound showed no appendiceal inflammation and a normal gallbladder. She received intravenous antibiotics and was discharged on oral antibiotics, but over the next few days her flank pain intensified and was accompanied by back and chest pain.

An MRI of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a 7.9 × 3.9 × 3.0 cm multilobulated right adrenal mass that was mildly heterogeneous and T2 hyperintense, grossly isointense to renal parenchyma on T1-weighted images, with surrounding inflammation tracking along the retroperitoneum. A rising white blood cell count (23 × 10^3/mL) and fever (101.0 °F) led to a presumptive diagnosis of pyelonephritis; she was started on intravenous ceftriaxone and transferred to the ICU.

In the ICU, her temperature was 99 °F, pulse 79 bpm, and blood pressure ranged from 147/81 to 166/97 mm Hg. Laboratory evaluation showed potassium 3.4 mmol/L, creatinine 0.47 mg/dL, AST 12 IU/L, ALT 8 IU/L, and alkaline phosphatase 55 IU/L. Hormonal studies revealed an aldosterone level of 9.1 ng/dL (nonpregnant reference, <31.0 ng/dL), plasma renin activity 2.9 ng/mL/h (normal, 0.5–4.0 ng/mL/h), random cortisol 17.2 μg/dL (normal, 7.0–23.0 μg/dL), and 24-hour urine free cortisol 43.6 μg/24 h (normal, <45.0 μg/24 h). Plasma metanephrine was 0.82 nmol/L (normal, 0.00–0.49 nmol/L) and normetanephrine 1.50 nmol/L (normal, 0.00–0.89 nmol/L). Twenty-four–hour urine metanephrine was 656 μg/d (normal, 39–143 μg/d) and normetanephrine was 1031 μg/d (normal, 109–393 μg/d).

Her pyelonephritis resolved with antibiotics. Given that the imaging findings were inconsistent with a typical adenoma, the differential included pheochromocytoma, adrenal cortical carcinoma, or adrenal hemorrhage. Outpatient biochemical testing showed normal fractionated 24-hour urine metanephrines: 24-hour urine metanephrine 85 μg/d (normal, 36–190 μg/day), 24-hour urine normetanephrine 415 μg/day (normal, 35–482 μg/day), and 24-hour urine total metanephrines 500 μg/day (normal, 115–695 μg/day). Plasma fractionated metanephrines were normal. Androstenedione, chromogranin A, and 11-deoxycortisol levels were within normal limits.

A man in his 20s with no prior hearing disorder was referred to the ENT clinic after two episodes of sudden left-ear hearing loss occurring within three months of receiving his second dose of the BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccine. On day 1 after vaccination, he developed progressive left-ear hearing loss lasting 10 days. Treatment with pseudoephedrine and fluticasone nasal spray for suspected allergic otitis media yielded no improvement. He then took cetirizine 10 mg daily from day 9 to 24, with near-normal hearing on an audiogram performed on day 26 (normal pure-tone averages, bilateral type A tympanograms, masked bone conduction). On day 31, he experienced recurrent left-ear hearing loss unresponsive to a 5-day course of cetirizine. A high-dose oral prednisone regimen (60 mg/day for 7 days followed by taper) was given for presumed Meniere’s disease but failed to improve his hearing. Sequential audiograms on days 44 and 60 demonstrated progressive low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in the left ear and a marginal decline in word recognition. Videonystagmography on day 76 showed 93% left-ear caloric weakness and 50% directional preponderance to the right; optokinetic testing revealed asymmetrical, reduced left-sided responses. Additional blood tests—including complete blood count with differential, rheumatoid factor, comprehensive metabolic panel, and lipid profile—were all within normal limits, and anti–SARS-CoV-2 antibodies were positive.
differential_diagnoses: other causes of sensorineural hearing loss

case_text:
A 45-year-old man was referred for evaluation of proteinuria. He had noted occult blood in his urine since age 24. At age 37, hypertension was diagnosed, and laboratory tests showed microscopic hematuria and proteinuria of 0.4 g/g creatinine. Over the next eight years, proteinuria gradually increased to 1.16 g/g creatinine, prompting hospital admission.

He denied episodes of gross hematuria or flank pain. He was born at term; birth weight was unknown. There was no family history of kidney disease. Medications included losartan (25 mg daily), amlodipine (5 mg daily), and febuxostat (20 mg daily). He weighed 98.7 kg and was 177 cm tall (BMI 31.5 kg/m^2) and had been overweight (BMI >25) since adolescence. Blood pressure was 140/78 mm Hg.

Physical examination was unremarkable. Urinalysis showed proteinuria of 0.7 g/g creatinine, 10–19 red blood cells per high-power field, and red-cell casts. Serum creatinine was 0.69 mg/dL, cystatin C 0.74 mg/L, albumin 4.1 g/dL, uric acid 6.3 mg/dL, LDL cholesterol 144 mg/dL, and HbA1c 5.5%. Estimated GFR was 97.6 mL/min/1.73 m^2. C-reactive protein, IgG, IgA, complement levels, and ANA were within normal limits. ECG and chest radiograph were normal. Renal ultrasonography showed bilaterally normal-sized kidneys with preserved cortical thickness.

With a clinical suspicion of IgA nephropathy, a kidney biopsy was performed.

A 59‐year‐old woman was referred after an incidental finding of a brain mass on CT performed for evaluation of labyrinthitis. She was asymptomatic, with no neurologic deficits. Her medical history included hypertension and diabetes mellitus; there was no history of seizures. Laboratory studies were unremarkable.

Noncontrast head CT showed a well‐circumscribed hypodense mass in the temporal horn of the right lateral ventricle, with tiny hyperdense foci suggestive of prior microhemorrhages. Brain MRI demonstrated a lobulated intraventricular lesion measuring approximately 4.0 × 2.8 × 2.7 cm. On T1‐weighted imaging, the mass was hypointense relative to gray matter; on T2‐weighted imaging, it was heterogeneously hyperintense, with mild adjacent temporal lobe hyperintensity. Susceptibility‐weighted imaging confirmed punctate hypointense foci within the lesion. There was no or only minimal enhancement after gadolinium administration. High‐resolution 3D‐CISS sequences revealed internal cystic components. Short‐echo‐time proton spectroscopy of the lesion showed a partially decreased N‐acetylaspartate peak, a normal choline peak, and an increased myoinositol peak.

A 40-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus and a 36–pack-year smoking history presented with severe bilateral lower-limb claudication (walking tolerance ≤ 100 m) and a non-healing ulcer on the right fifth toe. He had no palpable femoral pulses, monophasic distal Doppler signals bilaterally, and was neurologically intact preoperatively with normal strength and sensation. CT angiography demonstrated bilateral external iliac artery occlusion with patent internal and common iliac arteries and collateralized flow to the femorals. 

He underwent an aortobifemoral bypass via a transperitoneal approach under general anesthesia. The procedure lasted 4 hours without intraoperative hypotension, inotropes, or leg compression. Post-bypass Doppler signals in the anterior and posterior tibial arteries were biphasic. 

Twelve hours postoperatively, he reported inability to dorsiflex his left foot. Examination revealed 0/5 strength of left ankle dorsiflexion and eversion, numbness over the dorsum of the left foot, an absent left Achilles reflex, and preserved warmth and biphasic distal pulses. Over the next two days, left ankle dorsiflexion improved to 2/5. 

A spine MRI showed no disc herniation, cord compression, or other abnormalities. A nerve conduction study revealed severe chronic sensory motor axonal peripheral polyneuropathy.

A 22-year-old Caucasian man presented with sudden onset left-sided chest pain radiating to the shoulder tip. The pain was sharp, worsened by deep inspiration and lying supine, and was accompanied by dyspnea and fatigue. He denied cough, fever, or prodromal viral symptoms. 

His past medical history was notable for ulcerative colitis diagnosed at age 17, limited to proctitis until a recent flare extended to the splenic flexure. Twelve days before presentation, he had begun mesalazine 1.6 g twice daily alongside a tapering course of prednisolone 40 mg daily. He continued to have abdominal pain, increased bowel frequency, and bloody diarrhea on arrival. 

On examination, he was afebrile, normotensive, and had normal oxygen saturation. Cardiorespiratory and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Chest radiography showed clear lung fields. 

Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 19.46 × 10^9/L, D-dimer 1.81 mg/L, and troponin-T 209 ng/L (reference <14 ng/L); serum electrolytes were normal. A 12-lead ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm with concave ST-segment elevation in leads V4–V6 and biphasic T-waves. Twenty-four hours later, he reported persistent chest pain and a repeat ECG showed dynamic T-wave inversion in leads I, aVL, and V4–V6, with a peak troponin-T of 242 ng/L. 

He was initially treated as a suspected pulmonary embolus and transferred to the cardiology ward. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a non-dilated left ventricle with severe global systolic and diastolic dysfunction (mitral annular plane systolic excursion 4 mm, E/e′ 13) and normal right ventricular function; there was no pericardial effusion. He was started on ramipril and bisoprolol, clinically stabilized, and was discharged with plans for outpatient cardiac magnetic resonance imaging and heart-failure clinic follow-up.

A 7-year-old female presented with a 12-month history of progressive bilateral neck swelling, intermittent fever, night sweats, and weight loss. She had no known tuberculosis exposure or similar family history. On examination, she was pale and undernourished (BMI 10.24 kg/m2) with multiple rubbery, mobile, non-tender lymph nodes in the submandibular, submental, and posterior cervical regions (largest 4 × 3 cm), as well as inguinal nodes; no hepatosplenomegaly was noted. Vital signs were normal. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (14.57 × 10^9/L) with neutrophilia, microcytic hypochromic anemia (hemoglobin 7.7 g/dL; MCV 64.7 fL), and thrombocytosis (974 × 10^9/L); ESR was 36 mm/h, CRP 641.8 mg/L, and ferritin 256 ng/mL. Peripheral smear demonstrated hypochromia, anisocytosis, microcytosis, rouleaux formation, and no blasts. Gene-Xpert testing on lymph node aspirate and sputum was negative for Mycobacterium tuberculosis, as was urine LF-LAM. Chest radiography revealed bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. CT imaging of the neck, chest, and abdomen showed generalized lymphadenopathy without extranodal masses. She was started empirically on anti-tuberculous therapy.
